Alternating solids with liquids helps clear the oral cavity and facilitates swallowing by preventing food from accumulating, thereby reducing the chance of aspiration. Additionally, thicker liquids are often preferred over thin liquids because they are easier to control during swallowing.

(Correct Option 2). Alternating liquids with solids helps the client clear food from the mouth and throat, improving swallowing efficiency and reducing the risk of aspiration. This technique promotes safer eating by ensuring that food does not remain in the oral cavity.

(Option 1). Thin liquids are more difficult to control and increase the risk of aspiration in clients with dysphagia. Thickened liquids are generally recommended instead.

(Option 3). Foods that are primarily liquid may also increase aspiration risk, especially if they are thin in consistency, and do not provide adequate control during swallowing.

(Option 4). Food should be placed on the unaffected side of the mouth to facilitate better control and chewing. Placing food on the affected side increases the risk of pocketing and aspiration.

Educational Objective
In post-stroke dysphagia, alternating solids with liquids helps clear the airway and reduces aspiration risk. Avoid thin liquids and place food on the unaffected side to promote safe swallowing.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Stroke Management

answerChoiceList.choice: Giving the client thin liquids
Alternating liquids with solids
Giving foods that are primarily liquid
Placing food in the affected side of the client’s mouth

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Basic Care and Comfort

questionList.questionText: A nurse is preparing a teaching plan for the parents of an infant with a ventricular peritoneal shunt. Which of the following questions does the nurse plan to include?
questionList.explanationText:
Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt is used to treat hydrocephalus by diverting excess cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) from the ventricles of the brain to the peritoneal cavity. One of the most serious complications of a VP shunt is shunt malfunction or infection, which can lead to increased intracranial pressure (ICP). Early signs of increased ICP in infants include lethargy, irritability, poor feeding, vomiting, and changes in level of consciousness. Prompt recognition of these symptoms is critical to prevent neurological damage or life-threatening complications.

(Correct Option 1). Lethargy is an early and significant sign of increased intracranial pressure or shunt malfunction. Parents should be instructed to immediately notify the physician if the infant becomes lethargic, as this requires urgent medical evaluation.

(Option 2). Increased urine output is not related to the function of a VP shunt. The shunt diverts CSF to the peritoneal cavity, not the urinary system, so this is an incorrect expectation.

(Option 3). A bulging anterior fontanel during crying can be a normal transient finding due to increased intrathoracic pressure. It is persistent bulging at rest that is concerning, not when crying.

(Option 4). The infant should not be routinely positioned on the side of the shunt. Positioning should avoid pressure on the shunt site, especially immediately after surgery, to prevent complications.

Educational Objective
Infants with VP shunts are at risk for increased intracranial pressure due to shunt malfunction. Early signs such as lethargy require immediate medical attention to prevent serious complications.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Hydrocephalus and Shunt Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Call the physician if the infant is lethargic.
Expect increased urine output with the shunt.
Call the physician if the anterior fontanel bulges when the infant cries.
Position the infant on the side of the shunt for sleep.

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: A nurse answers the call bell of a client who has been fitted with an internal cervical radiation implant, and the client states that she thinks that the implant has fallen out. The nurse checks the client and sees the implant lying in the bed. Which action should the nurse take first?
questionList.explanationText:
Internal radiation therapy (brachytherapy) involves placing a radioactive implant directly into or near a tumor, such as in cervical cancer. These implants emit continuous radiation and pose a risk of radiation exposure to healthcare providers and others if not handled properly. When an implant becomes dislodged, the nurse must prioritize radiation safety principles: time, distance, and shielding. Direct contact with the implant should be avoided, and appropriate tools such as long-handled forceps should be used to maintain distance, while placing the implant in a lead-lined container provides necessary shielding to minimize radiation exposure.

(Correct Option 4). The nurse should immediately use long-handled forceps to pick up the radioactive implant and place it in a lead container. This action ensures maximum radiation safety by minimizing exposure through distance and shielding, which is the highest priority.

(Option 1). Calling the physician is appropriate after ensuring immediate safety, but it is not the first action. The nurse must first manage the radiation hazard.

(Option 2). Reinserting the implant is outside the nurse’s scope of practice and would expose the nurse to direct radiation, making it unsafe and inappropriate.

(Option 3). Picking up the implant with gloved hands does not provide adequate protection from radiation exposure. Gloves do not shield radiation, and this action violates safety protocols.

Educational Objective
In brachytherapy, if a radioactive implant becomes dislodged, the nurse must prioritize radiation safety by using forceps and placing the implant in a lead container to minimize exposure.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Radiation Safety and Brachytherapy

answerChoiceList.choice: Calling the physician
Reinserting the implant into the client’s vagina
Picking up the implant with gloved hands and placing it in sterile water
Using long-handled forceps to place the implant in a lead container

standards.description: Safe and Effective Care Environment: Safety and Infection Control

questionList.questionText: A nurse is reviewing this rhythm strip from a cardiac monitor. Which type of abnormal beats does the nurse recognize?
questionList.explanationText:
Premature ventricular contractions (PVCs) are early depolarizations originating from the ventricles rather than the sinoatrial (SA) node. They are characterized by wide, bizarre QRS complexes, absence of a preceding P wave, and an irregular rhythm. PVCs may occur in response to hypoxia, electrolyte imbalances, myocardial ischemia, or stress. While occasional PVCs may be benign, frequent or patterned PVCs can indicate underlying cardiac instability and require further evaluation. Recognizing these abnormal beats is important for timely intervention and prevention of more serious arrhythmias.

(Correct Option 4). PVCs are identified by premature, wide QRS complexes without preceding P waves and often followed by a compensatory pause. These features distinguish them from normal sinus beats and other arrhythmias, making this the correct interpretation.

(Option 1). Sinus bradycardia is characterized by a slow heart rate with normal P waves and QRS complexes. It does not present with premature or abnormal wide complexes.

(Option 2). Ventricular fibrillation is a life-threatening rhythm with chaotic, irregular waveforms and no identifiable P waves or QRS complexes. It appears completely disorganized, unlike isolated premature beats.

(Option 3). Ventricular tachycardia is a rapid rhythm with consecutive wide QRS complexes, typically sustained, rather than occasional premature beats seen in PVCs.

Educational Objective
PVCs are premature ventricular beats characterized by wide QRS complexes and absence of P waves. Recognizing PVCs is important for identifying potential cardiac instability and preventing progression to more serious arrhythmias.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Cardiac Dysrhythmias

answerChoiceList.choice: Sinus bradycardia
Ventricular fibrillation
Ventricular tachycardia
Premature ventricular contractions (PVCs)

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: A nurse notes that a client has ST-segment depression on the electrocardiogram (ECG) monitor. With which of the following serum potassium readings does the nurse associate this finding?
questionList.explanationText:
Potassium plays a critical role in maintaining cardiac electrical activity and proper myocardial conduction. Hypokalemia (low potassium levels) can lead to characteristic ECG changes such as ST-segment depression, flattened T waves, and the presence of U waves. These changes occur because low potassium levels alter the repolarization phase of cardiac cells, increasing the risk of dysrhythmias. Normal potassium levels range from 3.5 to 5.0 mEq/L, and values below this range indicate hypokalemia.

(Correct Option 1). A potassium level of 3.1 mEq/L indicates hypokalemia, which is associated with ST-segment depression and other ECG changes. This finding reflects altered cardiac repolarization and requires prompt correction to prevent arrhythmias.

(Option 2). A potassium level of 4.2 mEq/L is within the normal range and would not typically cause ECG abnormalities such as ST-segment depression.

(Option 3). A potassium level of 4.5 mEq/L is also within normal limits and is not associated with abnormal ECG changes.

(Option 4). A potassium level of 5.4 mEq/L indicates hyperkalemia, which is associated with ECG changes such as peaked T waves, widened QRS complexes, and possible heart block, not ST-segment depression.

Educational Objective
Hypokalemia causes ST-segment depression and increases the risk of cardiac dysrhythmias. Recognizing ECG changes associated with electrolyte imbalances is essential for prompt intervention.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Electrolyte Imbalances and ECG Changes

answerChoiceList.choice: 3.1 mEq/L
4.2 mEq/L
4.5 mEq/L
5.4 mEq/L

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: A nurse provides home care instructions to a client after a scleral buckling procedure. The nurse should tell the client:
questionList.explanationText:
Scleral buckling is a surgical procedure used to repair a detached retina by placing a silicone band around the eye to indent the sclera and relieve traction on the retina. Postoperatively, clients may experience localized inflammation, leading to redness and swelling of the eyelids and conjunctiva. These are expected findings due to surgical manipulation. However, patients must also be monitored for complications such as infection, increased intraocular pressure, or vision changes. Proper patient education helps distinguish between normal postoperative findings and symptoms requiring medical attention.

(Correct Option 4). Redness and swelling of the eyelids and conjunctiva are expected postoperative findings following scleral buckling due to inflammation and tissue manipulation. The client should be reassured that these symptoms are normal during recovery.

(Option 1). Strict bedrest for 48 hours is not typically required. While some activity restrictions may be advised, prolonged bedrest is not standard and may not be necessary.

(Option 2). Bloody drainage is not an expected finding and may indicate a complication such as hemorrhage. The client should report this immediately.

(Option 3). Vision is usually blurred initially after surgery and improves gradually. Immediate perfect vision is unrealistic and incorrect.

Educational Objective
After scleral buckling, mild redness and swelling are expected due to inflammation. Patients should be educated to recognize normal recovery findings versus signs of complications requiring prompt medical attention.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Retinal Detachment and Postoperative Care

answerChoiceList.choice: To maintain strict bedrest for 48 hours
To expect bloody drainage on the eye dressing
That vision will be perfectly clear immediately after surgery
That redness and swelling of the eyelids and conjunctiva are expected

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Basic Care and Comfort

questionList.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client with Crohn disease whose magnesium level is 1.0 mg/dL. Which assessment findings does the nurse expect to note? Select all that apply.
questionList.explanationText:
Hypomagnesemia (low magnesium levels) commonly occurs in clients with Crohn disease due to malabsorption and chronic diarrhea. Magnesium is essential for neuromuscular function and electrolyte balance, especially its interaction with calcium and potassium. Low magnesium levels lead to increased neuromuscular excitability, resulting in signs such as tetany, positive Trousseau sign, muscle cramps, and abdominal distention. The normal magnesium range is approximately 1.5–2.5 mg/dL, and a level of 1.0 mg/dL indicates significant deficiency. Recognizing these symptoms is critical to prevent complications such as cardiac dysrhythmias and seizures.

(Correct Option 2 and Option 3). Abdominal distention occurs due to altered smooth muscle function and gastrointestinal motility in hypomagnesemia. A positive Trousseau sign reflects increased neuromuscular excitability and is a classic sign associated with low magnesium and calcium levels.

(Option 1). Hypotension is more commonly associated with hypermagnesemia, not hypomagnesemia. Low magnesium typically leads to neuromuscular irritability rather than decreased blood pressure.

(Option 4). Skeletal muscle weakness is more characteristic of hypermagnesemia. Hypomagnesemia instead causes muscle hyperexcitability and cramps.

(Option 5). Decreased deep tendon reflexes are also seen in hypermagnesemia. In hypomagnesemia, reflexes are typically increased (hyperreflexia).

Educational Objective
Hypomagnesemia causes increased neuromuscular excitability, leading to signs like Trousseau sign and abdominal distention. It is important to distinguish these findings from hypermagnesemia, which presents with decreased reflexes and muscle weakness.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Electrolyte Imbalances

answerChoiceList.choice: Hypotension
Abdominal distention
Trousseau sign
Skeletal muscle weakness
Decreased deep tendon reflexes

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: A client has undergone creation of an Indiana pouch for urine diversion after cystectomy, and the nurse provides instructions about reservoir catheterization. The nurse tells the client:
questionList.explanationText:
An Indiana pouch is a type of continent urinary diversion created after cystectomy, using a segment of the intestine to form a reservoir for urine storage. The client must perform intermittent self-catheterization to empty the pouch because there is no continuous drainage like an ileal conduit. Initially, the pouch has a limited capacity and requires frequent emptying to prevent overdistention, leakage, and infection. Over time, the reservoir adapts and increases in capacity, allowing for less frequent catheterization. Proper education is essential to prevent complications such as urinary retention, infection, and trauma to the stoma.

(Correct Option 1). The client should be instructed to drain the reservoir every 2 to 3 hours initially because the pouch is newly formed and has a small capacity. Frequent emptying prevents overdistention and leakage, and helps the pouch gradually expand safely over time.

(Option 2). Mucus drainage is expected because the pouch is made from intestinal tissue, which naturally secretes mucus. This does not require notifying the physician unless it is excessive or accompanied by other symptoms.

(Option 3). Force should never be used when inserting a catheter, as it can cause trauma, bleeding, or perforation of the stoma or reservoir. Gentle insertion is always required.

(Option 4). A 26F catheter is too large and inappropriate for routine catheterization. Typically, smaller-sized catheters are used to prevent injury and discomfort.

Educational Objective
Clients with an Indiana pouch must perform frequent catheterization initially to prevent overdistention. Mucus drainage is normal, and catheterization should always be performed gently using appropriate-sized equipment.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Urinary Diversion and Ostomy Care

answerChoiceList.choice: To plan to drain the reservoir every 2 to 3 hours initially
That if mucus drains from the reservoir the physician should be contacted
That sometimes force is needed to insert the catheter into the reservoir
To obtain 26F catheters from the medical supply store for the irrigations

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Basic Care and Comfort

questionList.questionText: A nurse is caring for a client experiencing hyponatremia who was admitted to the medical-surgical unit with fluid-volume overload. For which clinical manifestations of this electrolyte imbalance does the nurse monitor this client? Select all that apply.
questionList.explanationText:
Hyponatremia is defined as a serum sodium level below 135 mEq/L and often occurs in conditions of fluid-volume overload due to dilution of sodium. Sodium is essential for maintaining neuromuscular function and fluid balance, and low levels lead to cellular swelling, particularly affecting the brain and muscles. This results in symptoms such as skeletal muscle weakness, fatigue, confusion, and increased gastrointestinal activity. Additionally, sodium imbalance affects neurological and gastrointestinal systems, leading to manifestations like hyperactive bowel sounds. Recognizing these signs early helps prevent complications such as seizures and cerebral edema.

(Correct Option 3 and Option 4). Skeletal muscle weakness occurs due to impaired neuromuscular transmission from low sodium levels. Hyperactive bowel sounds are also seen because sodium imbalance can increase gastrointestinal motility, making these findings consistent with hyponatremia.

(Option 1). A slow pulse is not a typical manifestation of hyponatremia; it is more commonly associated with other electrolyte imbalances or cardiac conditions.

(Option 2). Decreased urine output is related to fluid balance disorders but is not a direct or specific sign of hyponatremia itself.

(Option 5). Hyperactive deep tendon reflexes are more commonly associated with hypernatremia or other electrolyte disturbances, whereas hyponatremia may lead to normal or decreased reflex activity.

Educational Objective
Hyponatremia leads to neuromuscular and gastrointestinal changes such as muscle weakness and hyperactive bowel sounds. Early recognition is essential to prevent severe neurological complications.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Sodium Imbalance

answerChoiceList.choice: Slow pulse
Decreased urine output
Skeletal muscle weakness
Hyperactive bowel sounds
Hyperactive deep tendon reflexes

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: A nurse is caring for a hospitalized client who is undergoing peritoneal dialysis. The nurse notes that the outflow is less than the inflow on the first exchange. What should the nurse do first?
questionList.explanationText:
Peritoneal dialysis is a renal replacement therapy that uses the peritoneal membrane as a semipermeable membrane to remove waste products and excess fluid. During the process, dialysate is instilled into the abdomen (inflow) and later drained (outflow). Normally, the outflow should be approximately equal to or greater than the inflow due to ultrafiltration. When the outflow is less than the inflow, it may indicate a mechanical problem such as kinks in the tubing, catheter obstruction, or improper positioning. The nurse must first assess for simple and correctable causes before performing invasive interventions.

(Correct Option 3). The nurse should first check the system for kinks because this is the most common and easily correctable cause of decreased outflow. Identifying and correcting a mechanical obstruction ensures proper drainage and prevents unnecessary interventions.

(Option 1). Irrigating the catheter is a more invasive procedure and should only be done after simpler causes like kinks or positioning have been ruled out.

(Option 2). Repositioning the client can help improve outflow, but it is not the first step. The nurse should initially check for external mechanical issues such as tubing kinks.

(Option 4). Hanging the second exchange without addressing the decreased outflow can worsen fluid imbalance and does not correct the underlying problem.

Educational Objective
In peritoneal dialysis, decreased outflow should first be assessed for simple mechanical causes like kinks in tubing. Always address the least invasive and most easily correctable issues first.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Peritoneal Dialysis

answerChoiceList.choice: Irrigate the catheter
Reposition the client
Check the system for kinks
Hang the second exchange and continue to monitor the outflow

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: A nurse is assessing a client with AIDS for signs of Pneumocystis jiroveci infection. Which sign of the infection is the earliest manifestation?
questionList.explanationText:
Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia (PJP) is a common opportunistic infection seen in clients with AIDS due to severe immunosuppression and low CD4+ T-cell counts. This infection primarily affects the lungs, leading to progressive inflammation and impaired gas exchange. The earliest manifestation is typically a nonproductive (dry) cough, which occurs due to irritation of the alveoli without significant mucus production. As the disease progresses, clients may develop dyspnea on exertion, fever, and eventually dyspnea at rest, indicating worsening respiratory compromise. Early identification is critical to prevent progression to respiratory failure.

(Correct Option 4). A nonproductive cough is the earliest and most characteristic initial symptom of Pneumocystis jiroveci infection. It reflects early alveolar irritation and inflammation before significant impairment in oxygen exchange occurs.

(Option 1). Fever may develop as the infection progresses, but it is not typically the earliest sign. It appears after the inflammatory response becomes more pronounced.

(Option 2). Dyspnea at rest is a late and severe manifestation, indicating advanced disease and significant respiratory compromise.

(Option 3). Dyspnea on exertion occurs earlier than dyspnea at rest but still develops after the initial symptom of cough, reflecting worsening pulmonary involvement.

Educational Objective
Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia in clients with AIDS typically presents first with a nonproductive cough. Early recognition allows prompt treatment and prevents progression to severe respiratory distress.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Opportunistic Infections in AIDS

answerChoiceList.choice: Fever
Dyspnea at rest
Dyspnea on exertion
Nonproductive cough

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: A client with a spinal cord injury suddenly complains of a pounding headache. The nurse quickly assesses the client and notes that the client is diaphoretic, that his blood pressure has increased, and that his heart rate has slowed. Suspecting that the client is experiencing autonomic dysreflexia, the nurse elevates the head of the client’s bed and immediately:
questionList.explanationText:
Autonomic dysreflexia is a life-threatening condition seen in clients with spinal cord injuries above T6. It results from an exaggerated sympathetic nervous system response to a noxious stimulus below the level of injury. Common triggers include bladder distention, bowel impaction, or skin irritation. The condition causes severe hypertension, bradycardia, headache, diaphoresis, and flushing. Immediate nursing actions focus on removing the triggering stimulus to prevent complications such as stroke, seizures, or cardiac arrest.

(Correct Option C). Checking the client’s bladder for distention is the priority intervention because bladder distention is the most common cause of autonomic dysreflexia. Relief of urinary retention (e.g., catheterization) can quickly reduce the stimulus and lower blood pressure. Addressing the underlying cause is essential before other interventions.

(Option A). Documenting the event is important but is not a priority during an acute emergency. Immediate intervention to remove the cause of autonomic dysreflexia must occur first to prevent serious complications.

(Option B). Notifying the healthcare provider may be necessary, but it is not the first action. Nursing interventions aimed at identifying and eliminating the triggering stimulus should be performed immediately.

(Option D). Checking for an antihypertensive prescription may be appropriate if initial measures fail, but medications are secondary to removing the cause. Treating the trigger often resolves the condition without pharmacologic intervention.

Educational Objective
Autonomic dysreflexia is a medical emergency requiring rapid identification and removal of the triggering stimulus. Bladder distention is the most common cause, and relieving it is the priority intervention to prevent severe complications.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Spinal Cord Injury & Autonomic Dysreflexia

answerChoiceList.choice: Documents the event
Notifies the healthcare provider
Checks the client’s bladder for distention
Checks to see whether the client has a prescription for an antihypertensive

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: A client who experiences frequent episodes of chest pain is admitted to the hospital for cardiac monitoring. The client suddenly complains of chest pain, and the nurse obtains a 12-lead electrocardiogram (ECG). Which of the following findings would the nurse expect to note in the event of an ischemic episode?
questionList.explanationText:
Myocardial ischemia occurs when there is a reduction in coronary blood flow, leading to inadequate oxygen supply to the heart muscle. This results in metabolic changes and altered electrical activity, which are reflected on an ECG. One of the hallmark ECG changes during ischemia is ST-segment depression, indicating subendocardial ischemia. Other changes may include T-wave inversion, but ST depression is the most classic finding during an acute ischemic episode without infarction. Early recognition is essential to prevent progression to myocardial infarction and irreversible damage.

(Correct Option B). ST-segment depression is a classic indicator of myocardial ischemia. It reflects insufficient oxygen supply to the heart muscle, particularly affecting the subendocardial region. This ECG finding is commonly seen during episodes of angina pectoris and signals the need for immediate intervention to restore adequate perfusion.

(Option A). Peaked T waves are typically associated with hyperkalemia, not myocardial ischemia. Although T-wave changes can occur in ischemia, they are usually inverted rather than peaked.

(Option C). A widened QRS complex is usually related to bundle branch blocks or ventricular conduction delays, not specifically to ischemia. It does not directly indicate reduced coronary perfusion.

(Option D). An isolated premature ventricular contraction (PVC) is an ectopic beat that may occur in various conditions, including stress or electrolyte imbalance. It is not a specific indicator of myocardial ischemia.

Educational Objective
Myocardial ischemia is characterized on ECG by ST-segment depression due to reduced coronary blood flow. Recognizing this early sign helps prevent progression to myocardial infarction and guides timely intervention.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Myocardial Ischemia & ECG Interpretation

answerChoiceList.choice: Peaked T waves
ST-segment depression
Widened QRS complex
An isolated premature ventricular contraction (PVC)

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: A nurse arrives at the scene of a code and begins to assist with cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) of an adult. The nurse delivers compressions by pushing down on the chest to a depth of:
questionList.explanationText:
Cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) is an emergency life-saving technique used when the heart stops beating. Effective CPR requires proper compression depth, rate, and recoil to maintain adequate blood circulation and oxygen delivery to vital organs. According to current American Heart Association (AHA) guidelines, adult chest compressions should be performed at a depth of at least 2 inches (5 cm) but not more than 2.4 inches. Adequate depth ensures effective cardiac output, while insufficient depth leads to poor perfusion and decreased survival outcomes.

(Correct Option C). A compression depth of 2 inches is the recommended standard for adult CPR. This depth provides sufficient pressure to compress the heart between the sternum and spine, allowing blood to circulate effectively. Maintaining this depth improves oxygenation of the brain and vital organs and increases the chances of survival during cardiac arrest.

(Option A). A depth of 1 inch is too shallow for adult CPR and will not generate adequate blood flow. Ineffective compressions can result in poor perfusion and decreased survival rates.

(Option B). A depth of 1½ inches is still insufficient for adults. Although it may be appropriate for children, it does not meet the recommended guidelines for adult CPR.

(Option D). A depth of 4 inches is excessively deep and can cause serious injuries such as rib fractures or internal organ damage. It exceeds safe and recommended limits.

Educational Objective
Effective adult CPR requires chest compressions of at least 2 inches in depth. Proper compression depth is critical to maintain adequate circulation and improve survival outcomes during cardiac arrest.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals of Nursing
questionList.title: Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR)

answerChoiceList.choice: 1 inch
1½ inches
2 inches
4 inches

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Basic Care and Comfort

questionList.questionText: Mastitis is diagnosed in a client who recently gave birth. The nurse tells the woman that:
questionList.explanationText:
Mastitis is an inflammation of the breast tissue, commonly occurring in lactating women due to milk stasis and bacterial infection, often caused by organisms such as Staphylococcus aureus. It presents with breast pain, redness, warmth, swelling, fever, and flu-like symptoms. Management includes continued breastfeeding or pumping to empty the breast, use of antibiotics if infection is present, and supportive measures like moist heat to improve circulation and milk flow. Proper breast emptying is essential to prevent worsening of the condition.

(Correct Option D). Moist heat helps to increase blood flow and promote milk let-down, which can relieve obstruction and discomfort. Applying warm compresses before breastfeeding or pumping enhances circulation and drainage of milk, thereby improving symptoms and aiding recovery.

(Option A). Wearing a supportive bra actually helps to reduce discomfort and provide breast support. Not wearing a bra may increase pain due to lack of support.

(Option B). Antibiotics are commonly used when mastitis is associated with bacterial infection. They are an important part of treatment to prevent complications such as abscess formation.

(Option C). Breastfeeding should continue during mastitis unless contraindicated. Continued emptying of the breast helps resolve the condition and prevents further milk stasis.

Educational Objective
Mastitis management includes continued breastfeeding, antibiotics if needed, and supportive measures such as moist heat to improve circulation and milk flow. Breast emptying is key to recovery.
questionList.subject: Maternal-Newborn Nursing
questionList.title: Postpartum Complications: Mastitis

answerChoiceList.choice: Wearing a bra will increase the discomfort
Antibiotics are not usually used to treat this disorder
Breastfeeding must be discontinued until the condition resolves
Moist heat will increase circulation and may be used before the breasts are emptied

standards.description: Health Promotion and Maintenance

questionList.questionText: A nurse is obtaining subjective data from the mother of a child admitted to the hospital with a diagnosis of intussusception. Which of the following occurrences does the nurse expect the mother to report?
questionList.explanationText:
Intussusception is a serious condition in which a portion of the intestine telescopes into an adjacent segment, leading to bowel obstruction and compromised blood supply. This results in ischemia, inflammation, and possible necrosis of the affected bowel. A hallmark clinical manifestation is the passage of "currant jelly" stools, which consist of bloody mucus due to sloughing of the intestinal mucosa. Other symptoms include intermittent severe abdominal pain, vomiting, lethargy, and diarrhea. Early recognition is critical to prevent complications such as bowel perforation and shock.

(Correct Option D). Bloody mucus stools and diarrhea are classic signs of intussusception. The presence of "currant jelly" stools reflects intestinal ischemia and mucosal damage, making this a key diagnostic indicator reported by caregivers.

(Option A). Scleral jaundice indicates liver dysfunction or elevated bilirubin levels, not a gastrointestinal obstruction like intussusception.

(Option B). Projectile vomiting is commonly associated with pyloric stenosis, not intussusception. While vomiting may occur, it is not typically projectile in nature.

(Option C). Hard, pale stools are more indicative of constipation or biliary obstruction, not the bloody, mucus-filled stools seen in intussusception.

Educational Objective
Intussusception presents with "currant jelly" stools (bloody mucus), abdominal pain, and possible vomiting. Recognizing this hallmark sign is essential for early diagnosis and prevention of serious complications.
questionList.subject: Pediatric Nursing
questionList.title: Intussusception

answerChoiceList.choice: Scleral jaundice
Projectile vomiting
Hard, pale stools
Bloody mucus stools and diarrhea

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: A nurse is working in the emergency department. Which of the following clients should be assessed first?
questionList.explanationText:
Emergency department prioritization follows the principles of ABCs (Airway, Breathing, Circulation) and hemodynamic stability. Conditions that affect cardiac output, oxygen delivery, or can rapidly deteriorate are prioritized first. Atrial fibrillation, especially with a rapid ventricular response, can impair cardiac efficiency and lead to decreased perfusion, hypotension, or risk of thromboembolism. Therefore, patients with unstable cardiac rhythms require immediate evaluation to prevent complications such as stroke or heart failure.

(Correct Option 4). A client with new-onset atrial fibrillation with a rate of 118 beats/min requires immediate assessment because this indicates a cardiac dysrhythmia that can compromise circulation and perfusion. Rapid atrial fibrillation can quickly deteriorate into more severe complications, making it the highest priority.

(Option 1). New-onset dizziness may indicate a neurological or vestibular issue, but without signs of instability, it is not immediately life-threatening compared to cardiac dysrhythmia.

(Option 2). A recent ear injury requires assessment, but unless there are signs of severe trauma or bleeding, it is less urgent than a potentially unstable cardiac condition.

(Option 3). Nausea and vomiting for 12 hours may lead to dehydration or electrolyte imbalance, but it is typically not as immediately life-threatening as an acute cardiac rhythm disturbance.

Educational Objective
In emergency care, prioritize clients based on ABCs and potential for rapid deterioration. Cardiac dysrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation with rapid rate require immediate intervention due to risk of compromised perfusion and life-threatening complications.
questionList.subject: Fundamentals
questionList.title: Prioritization and Triage

answerChoiceList.choice: A client with new-onset dizziness
A client admitted with a recent ear injury
A client who has been experiencing nausea and vomiting for 12 hours
A client with new-onset atrial fibrillation with a rate of 118 beats/min

standards.description: Management of Care

questionList.questionText: An ambulatory care nurse is providing home care instructions to the mother of a child who had a tonsillectomy. The nurse determines that the mother needs further instruction if she indicates that she will:
questionList.explanationText:
After a tonsillectomy, the primary nursing concerns include prevention of hemorrhage, pain management, and maintenance of hydration. The surgical site in the throat is highly vascular, making it prone to bleeding, especially if irritation or trauma occurs. Activities or actions that increase pressure in the oropharynx, such as coughing, straining, or using a straw, can dislodge clots and lead to postoperative hemorrhage. Additionally, avoiding acidic or irritating foods helps protect the healing tissue, while maintaining hydration helps reduce foul odor caused by healing tissue.

(Correct Option 2). The use of a straw is contraindicated after a tonsillectomy because it creates negative pressure in the mouth and throat, which can disrupt the surgical site and lead to bleeding or hemorrhage. This indicates the mother needs further instruction, as this practice is unsafe.

(Option 1). Avoiding citrus juices is correct because acidic fluids can irritate the surgical site and cause discomfort or bleeding.

(Option 3). Administering acetaminophen (Tylenol) is appropriate for pain management, as it does not increase bleeding risk like NSAIDs or aspirin.

(Option 4). Providing extra fluids is beneficial because it helps maintain hydration and reduces foul mouth odor, which is common during healing.

Educational Objective
After tonsillectomy, avoid actions that increase pressure in the throat, such as using straws, to prevent hemorrhage. Encourage hydration, use safe analgesics, and avoid irritating foods to promote healing.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Postoperative Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Avoid giving citrus juices to her child
Have her child use a straw to make drinking easier
Give acetaminophen (Tylenol) to her child for discomfort
Give her child extra fluids to relieve a foul odor from the mouth

standards.description: Health Promotion and Maintenance

questionList.questionText: A nurse in the labor room is performing a vaginal assessment of a pregnant client who is in active labor. The nurse notes that the umbilical cord is protruding from the vagina and immediately:
questionList.explanationText:
Umbilical cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency where the umbilical cord descends before or alongside the presenting part of the fetus. This condition leads to compression of the cord, which can significantly reduce or completely obstruct fetal oxygen supply, resulting in fetal hypoxia and possible fetal death if not managed promptly. Immediate nursing interventions focus on relieving pressure on the cord and improving fetal perfusion. Positioning the client to reduce pressure is critical while preparing for definitive delivery, usually by emergency cesarean section.

(Correct Option 3). Placing the client in the knee-chest position helps shift the fetus off the umbilical cord, thereby relieving compression and improving blood flow to the fetus. This is the immediate priority intervention to prevent fetal hypoxia while further emergency measures are arranged.

(Option 1). Attempting to push the cord back into the vagina is inappropriate because it can cause vasospasm and further compromise fetal circulation.

(Option 2). Preparing for a cesarean delivery is necessary but is not the immediate first action. The priority is to relieve cord compression first.

(Option 4). Administering a tocolytic may help reduce uterine contractions, but it is not the initial priority intervention compared to positioning to relieve cord compression.

Educational Objective
Umbilical cord prolapse is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate action to relieve cord compression. Positioning the client in a knee-chest or Trendelenburg position is the priority to maintain fetal oxygenation before definitive delivery.
questionList.subject: Maternity Nursing
questionList.title: Labor and Delivery Emergencies

answerChoiceList.choice: Pushes the cord gently back into the vagina
Prepares the client for cesarean delivery
Places the client in the knee-chest position
Prepares to administer a tocolytic medication

standards.description: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: A client has an arteriovenous fistula in place for hemodialysis. What should the nurse do to assess the patency of the fistula?
questionList.explanationText:
An arteriovenous (AV) fistula is a surgically created connection between an artery and a vein used for hemodialysis access. The high-pressure arterial blood flow into the vein creates a characteristic turbulent blood flow, which produces a palpable thrill (vibration) and an audible bruit. Assessing fistula patency is essential to ensure adequate blood flow for dialysis and to prevent complications such as thrombosis or occlusion. The presence of a thrill indicates that the fistula is functioning properly, while absence suggests possible blockage or failure.

(Correct Option 3). Palpating for a vibrating sensation (thrill) at the fistula site is the correct method to assess patency. This indicates adequate blood flow through the fistula and confirms that it is functioning properly for dialysis.

(Option 1). Irrigating the fistula with normal saline is inappropriate because AV fistulas are not accessed or flushed routinely like IV lines, and this could cause infection or damage.

(Option 2). Infusing saline into the fistula is incorrect because fistulas are not used for routine fluid administration and doing so may compromise the access site.

(Option 4). Flushing with heparin is not appropriate for AV fistulas; unlike central lines, fistulas maintain patency through natural blood flow and do not require flushing.

Educational Objective
To assess AV fistula patency, palpate for a thrill and auscultate for a bruit. These findings confirm adequate blood flow, while absence may indicate thrombosis or occlusion requiring immediate attention.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Hemodialysis Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Irrigate the fistula with 3 mL of normal saline solution
Infuse 50 mL of normal saline once per 24 hours
Palpate for a vibrating sensation at the fistula site
Flush the fistula with 1 mL of heparin solution once per shift

standards.description: Physiological Adaptation

questionList.questionText: A nurse attending a recertification course in basic life support (BLS) for healthcare professionals is practicing BLS on an infant mannequin. Where does the nurse place the fingers to assess the infant’s pulse?
questionList.explanationText:
In basic life support (BLS) for infants, accurate assessment of the heart rate is critical to determine the need for cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR). Unlike adults, infants have smaller and more delicate anatomical structures, making certain pulse sites more reliable. The brachial artery, located in the antecubital fossa of the arm, is the preferred site for assessing pulse in infants because it is more accessible and provides a more reliable indication of circulation. Proper technique ensures timely recognition of bradycardia or cardiac arrest, which are common causes of pediatric emergencies.

(Correct Option 4). The nurse should place fingers in the antecubital fossa of the arm to palpate the brachial pulse. This site is recommended in infants because it is easier to locate and provides an accurate assessment of cardiac activity.

(Option 1). The neck (carotid artery) is not typically used in infants because it is difficult to palpate and may compress the airway or vagus nerve.

(Option 2). The wrist (radial pulse) is unreliable in infants due to weak peripheral circulation and difficulty in palpation.

(Option 3). The area behind the knee (popliteal pulse) is not used in BLS assessment and is difficult to access quickly in an emergency.

Educational Objective
In infant BLS, the brachial pulse at the antecubital area is the preferred site for pulse assessment. Accurate and rapid assessment of heart rate is essential for initiating appropriate resuscitation measures.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Basic Life Support

answerChoiceList.choice: Neck
Wrist
Behind the knee
Antecubital fossa of the arm

standards.description: Physiological Adaptation

questionList.questionText: A nurse is conducting an assessment of a client with mild preeclampsia. Which sign indicates improvement in the client’s condition?
questionList.explanationText:
Preeclampsia is a pregnancy-related condition characterized by hypertension and proteinuria resulting from abnormal placental perfusion and endothelial dysfunction. It can progress to severe complications such as eclampsia, renal failure, and cerebral edema. Indicators of worsening condition include increasing blood pressure, rising protein levels in urine, elevated BUN levels, and neurological symptoms like headache. Improvement is indicated by reduction in these abnormal findings, particularly a decrease in proteinuria and stabilization of blood pressure.

(Correct Option 1). Trace protein in the urine indicates decreased proteinuria, which reflects improvement in kidney function and overall condition. This suggests that the severity of preeclampsia is reducing.

(Option 2). A BUN of 40 mg/dL is elevated and indicates impaired renal function, suggesting worsening rather than improvement.

(Option 3). A blood pressure of 148/94 mm Hg remains elevated and indicates ongoing hypertension, which is not a sign of improvement.

(Option 4). A complaint of headache suggests possible cerebral involvement and worsening condition, not improvement.

Educational Objective
Improvement in preeclampsia is indicated by decreasing proteinuria and stabilization of blood pressure. Worsening signs include elevated BUN, persistent hypertension, and neurological symptoms such as headache.
questionList.subject: Maternity Nursing
questionList.title: Hypertensive Disorders in Pregnancy

answerChoiceList.choice: Trace protein in the urine
Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) of 40 mg/dL
Blood pressure 148/94 mm Hg
Complaint of headache

standards.description: Physiological Adaptation

questionList.questionText: A nurse is caring for a hospitalized child with a diagnosis of Kawasaki disease. During the subacute phase, the nurse monitors the child closely for:
questionList.explanationText:
Kawasaki disease is an acute systemic vasculitis that primarily affects young children and can lead to serious cardiac complications, especially involving the coronary arteries. The disease progresses through phases: the acute phase (high fever and inflammation), the subacute phase (resolution of fever but ongoing inflammation), and the convalescent phase. During the subacute phase, the greatest risk is the development of coronary artery aneurysms, which can lead to myocardial dysfunction and congestive heart failure (CHF). Therefore, close monitoring of cardiac status is essential.

(Correct Option 4). During the subacute phase, the nurse should monitor for signs of congestive heart failure (CHF) because inflammation of the coronary arteries can impair cardiac function. Early detection of CHF helps prevent serious complications such as cardiac failure or myocardial infarction.

(Option 1). Bleeding is not a primary concern of Kawasaki disease unless related to medication side effects, but it is not the main focus during the subacute phase.

(Option 2). A high fever is characteristic of the acute phase, not the subacute phase, where fever typically subsides.

(Option 3). Failure to thrive may occur in chronic conditions but is not the priority concern during the subacute phase of Kawasaki disease.

Educational Objective
Kawasaki disease poses a high risk for coronary artery complications. During the subacute phase, nurses must closely monitor for signs of cardiac involvement, especially congestive heart failure, to prevent life-threatening outcomes.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Pediatric Cardiac Disorders

answerChoiceList.choice: Bleeding
A high fever
Failure to thrive
Signs of congestive heart failure (CHF)

standards.description: Physiological Adaptation

questionList.questionText: A nurse is reviewing the assessment findings and laboratory results of a child with a diagnosis of new-onset glomerulonephritis. Which of the following findings would the nurse expect to note?
questionList.explanationText:
Glomerulonephritis is an inflammatory condition of the glomeruli in the kidneys, often occurring after a streptococcal infection. This inflammation leads to decreased glomerular filtration rate (GFR), resulting in fluid retention, sodium retention, and impaired excretion of waste products. As a result, clients commonly develop hypertension, edema, hematuria (tea- or cola-colored urine), and proteinuria. The retention of fluid increases intravascular volume, which directly contributes to elevated blood pressure.

(Correct Option 1). Hypertension is an expected finding in glomerulonephritis due to fluid overload and sodium retention. The kidneys are unable to regulate fluid balance effectively, leading to increased blood volume and elevated blood pressure.

(Option 2). Low serum potassium is not expected; instead, hyperkalemia may occur due to decreased renal excretion.

(Option 3). Although creatinine may be elevated, it is not the most characteristic or expected early clinical finding compared to hypertension.

(Option 4). Urine in glomerulonephritis is typically dark, smoky, or tea-colored due to hematuria, not cloudy yellow.

Educational Objective
Glomerulonephritis leads to decreased kidney function and fluid retention, resulting in hypertension, edema, and hematuria. Recognizing these hallmark signs is essential for early identification and management.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Pediatric Renal Disorders

answerChoiceList.choice: Hypertension
Low serum potassium
Increased creatinine level
Cloudy yellow urine

standards.description: Physiological Adaptation

questionList.questionText: A nurse is monitoring a client with hyperparathyroidism for signs of hypercalcemia. For which of the following clinical manifestations, associated with this electrolyte imbalance, does the nurse assess the client? Select all that apply.
questionList.explanationText:
Hyperparathyroidism leads to excessive secretion of parathyroid hormone (PTH), which increases serum calcium levels by promoting bone resorption, increasing intestinal absorption, and enhancing renal reabsorption of calcium. Hypercalcemia results in decreased neuromuscular excitability, causing symptoms such as muscle weakness, fatigue, and hyporeflexia. It also affects the kidneys, leading to polyuria due to decreased ability to concentrate urine. Classic signs of hypercalcemia are often summarized as "bones, stones, groans, and psychic overtones".

(Correct Option 2 and Option 3). Muscle weakness occurs due to reduced neuromuscular excitability caused by elevated calcium levels. Increased urine output (polyuria) occurs because hypercalcemia interferes with the kidneys' ability to concentrate urine, leading to fluid loss.

(Option 1). Paresthesias are associated with hypocalcemia, not hypercalcemia, due to increased neuromuscular excitability.

(Option 4). Chvostek sign is a classic sign of hypocalcemia, not hypercalcemia.

(Option 5). Hyperactive deep tendon reflexes occur in hypocalcemia, whereas hypercalcemia typically causes diminished reflexes.

Educational Objective
Hypercalcemia, commonly seen in hyperparathyroidism, leads to decreased neuromuscular activity, causing muscle weakness and polyuria. In contrast, hypocalcemia presents with increased neuromuscular excitability such as paresthesias and hyperreflexia.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Electrolyte Imbalances

answerChoiceList.choice: Paresthesias
Muscle weakness
Increased urine output
Chvostek sign
Hyperactive deep tendon reflexes

standards.description: Physiological Adaptation

questionList.questionText: A client who experienced a brain attack (stroke) exhibits right-sided unilateral neglect. The nurse caring for this client plans to place the client’s personal care items:
questionList.explanationText:
Unilateral neglect is a neurological deficit often seen after a stroke, particularly involving the parietal lobe. It results in the client being unaware of or ignoring one side of the body or environment, in this case the right side. Nursing care focuses on safety and gradual stimulation of the affected side to promote neurological recovery. By placing items on the affected side, the nurse encourages the client to use and acknowledge that side, which helps improve awareness and function over time.

(Correct Option 2). Placing personal care items within reach on the right (neglected) side encourages the client to turn toward and use the affected side. This promotes sensory stimulation and aids in rehabilitation of unilateral neglect.

(Option 1). Placing items on the left (unaffected side) reinforces neglect of the right side and does not promote recovery.

(Option 3). Placing items out of reach on the left side does not promote use of the affected side and may cause frustration.

(Option 4). Placing items out of reach on the right side may increase frustration and does not support safe or effective rehabilitation.

Educational Objective
In clients with unilateral neglect after stroke, place objects on the affected side to encourage awareness and use of that side, promoting neurological recovery and functional independence.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Stroke Rehabilitation

answerChoiceList.choice: Within the client’s reach on the left side
Within the client’s reach on the right side
Just out of the client’s reach on the left side
Just out of the client’s reach on the right side

standards.description: Physiological Adaptation

questionList.questionText: A nurse is caring for a child with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes mellitus who is receiving insulin. The child suddenly exhibits tachycardia and begins to sweat and tremble, and the nurse determines that the child is experiencing a hypoglycemic reaction. The nurse would immediately give the child:
questionList.explanationText:
Hypoglycemia occurs when blood glucose levels fall below normal, often as a result of excess insulin, delayed meals, or increased activity. Early signs include tachycardia, diaphoresis, tremors, and irritability, caused by activation of the sympathetic nervous system. Immediate treatment involves administering a rapid-acting source of glucose to quickly raise blood sugar levels and prevent progression to seizures or loss of consciousness. The recommended intervention is approximately 15 grams of fast-acting carbohydrate, such as fruit juice.

(Correct Option 4). Giving ½ cup of fruit juice provides a rapid source of simple carbohydrates that is quickly absorbed into the bloodstream, effectively raising blood glucose levels in a hypoglycemic episode.

(Option 1). A sugar cube may provide glucose, but it is not as reliable or fast-acting as a measured liquid carbohydrate source.

(Option 2). A teaspoon of sugar provides insufficient glucose to adequately treat hypoglycemia.

(Option 3). Diet cola does not contain sugar and therefore will not increase blood glucose levels.

Educational Objective
Hypoglycemia requires immediate treatment with fast-acting carbohydrates such as fruit juice. Early recognition and prompt intervention prevent severe complications like seizures or coma.
questionList.subject: Pediatrics
questionList.title: Diabetes Mellitus Management

answerChoiceList.choice: A sugar cube
A teaspoon of sugar
½ cup of diet cola
½ cup of fruit juice

standards.description: Physiological Adaptation

questionList.questionText: A nurse is providing discharge instructions to a client after outpatient surgery for cataract removal. The nurse determines that the client needs additional instructions if the client indicates that he will:
questionList.explanationText:
Cataract surgery involves removal of the cloudy lens and insertion of an artificial intraocular lens. Postoperative care focuses on preventing increased intraocular pressure (IOP), avoiding infection, and promoting healing. Clients are instructed to avoid activities that increase pressure such as bending, lifting, or straining. Mild discomfort is expected, but severe pain, nausea, or vomiting are abnormal and may indicate increased IOP or complications such as hemorrhage or infection. Early recognition of complications is critical to prevent vision loss.

(Correct Option 4). Expecting pain, nausea, and vomiting is incorrect because these symptoms are not normal after cataract surgery and may indicate increased intraocular pressure or other complications. The client should report these symptoms immediately.

(Option 1). Limiting activity for 24 hours is appropriate to prevent strain and promote healing after surgery.

(Option 2). Taking acetaminophen for mild discomfort is appropriate and safe because it does not increase bleeding risk.

(Option 3). Leaving the eye patch in place until follow-up is correct to protect the eye from injury and infection.

Educational Objective
After cataract surgery, mild discomfort is expected, but severe pain, nausea, or vomiting are warning signs of complications such as increased intraocular pressure and must be reported immediately.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Postoperative Eye Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Limit activity for 24 hours
Take acetaminophen (Tylenol) for discomfort
Leave the eye patch in place until he has been seen by the physician
Expect to experience pain, nausea, and vomiting after the procedure

standards.description: Physiological Adaptation

questionList.questionText: A nurse notes that a client’s serum potassium level is 5.8 mEq/L. The nurse interprets this as an expected finding in the client with:
questionList.explanationText:
Hyperkalemia is defined as a serum potassium level greater than 5.0 mEq/L and can lead to serious cardiac dysrhythmias. Potassium balance is primarily regulated by the kidneys under the influence of aldosterone, a hormone produced by the adrenal cortex. In conditions where aldosterone is deficient, such as Addison disease, potassium is not adequately excreted, leading to potassium retention and elevated serum levels. Conversely, conditions that involve fluid loss or use of certain medications often lead to hypokalemia rather than hyperkalemia.

(Correct Option 3). Addison disease is characterized by adrenal insufficiency, resulting in decreased production of aldosterone. This leads to reduced potassium excretion by the kidneys and subsequent hyperkalemia, making this the expected finding.

(Option 1). Diarrhea causes loss of potassium through the gastrointestinal tract, leading to hypokalemia, not hyperkalemia.

(Option 2). Wound drainage results in fluid and electrolyte loss, typically causing hypokalemia.

(Option 4). Loop diuretics increase potassium excretion by the kidneys, commonly leading to hypokalemia, not hyperkalemia.

Educational Objective
Hyperkalemia is commonly associated with conditions involving decreased aldosterone, such as Addison disease. Recognizing causes of electrolyte imbalances is essential for safe patient management and prevention of complications.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Electrolyte Imbalances

answerChoiceList.choice: Diarrhea
Wound drainage
Addison disease
Heart failure being treated with loop diuretics

standards.description: Physiological Adaptation

questionList.questionText: A woman in labor suddenly complains of abdominal tenderness and pain and states that she felt as though “something ripped.” For which manifestations does the nurse, suspecting uterine rupture, assess the client? Select all that apply.
questionList.explanationText:
Uterine rupture is a life-threatening obstetric emergency in which the uterine wall tears, often during labor. This leads to massive internal hemorrhage, loss of uterine integrity, and compromised fetal oxygenation. Classic signs include sudden severe abdominal pain, a tearing sensation, cessation of contractions, and fetal distress. As the fetus may be expelled into the abdominal cavity, fetal heart tones may become absent. Maternal signs can include hypotension, shock, and referred chest or shoulder pain due to diaphragmatic irritation from internal bleeding.

(Correct Option 2 and Option 3). Severe chest pain may occur due to referred pain from internal bleeding irritating the diaphragm. Absence of fetal heart tones is a critical sign indicating fetal compromise or death due to loss of uterine integrity and oxygen supply.

(Option 1). Bradypnea is not a typical sign of uterine rupture; respiratory changes are more commonly related to shock, not slowed breathing.

(Option 4). Increased blood pressure is incorrect; uterine rupture usually leads to hypotension due to hemorrhage.

(Option 5). Increased frequency of contractions is incorrect; contractions often decrease or stop after rupture due to loss of uterine muscle integrity.

Educational Objective
Uterine rupture is a medical emergency characterized by sudden severe pain and fetal distress. Key signs include absence of fetal heart tones and symptoms of internal bleeding, requiring immediate intervention.
questionList.subject: Maternity Nursing
questionList.title: Labor and Delivery Emergencies

answerChoiceList.choice: Bradypnea
Severe chest pain
Absence of fetal heart tones
Increased blood pressure
Increased frequency of uterine contractions

standards.description: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: A nurse is having dinner at a restaurant when a man sitting at the next table collapses and falls to the floor. The nurse yells for help and quickly assesses the client, noting that the client is not breathing and does not have a pulse. The nurse initiates cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) immediately, and the restaurant manager rushes to the scene with an automatic external defibrillator (AED). What should the nurse do next?
questionList.explanationText:
Cardiac arrest occurs when there is a sudden cessation of cardiac activity, leading to absence of pulse and respiration. The most common initial rhythms are ventricular fibrillation (VF) or pulseless ventricular tachycardia (VT), which are shockable rhythms. According to Basic Life Support (BLS) guidelines, early defibrillation is the most critical intervention to restore a viable heart rhythm. The sequence emphasizes early CPR and rapid use of an AED as soon as it becomes available to improve survival outcomes.

(Correct Option 1). The nurse should immediately use the AED because early defibrillation is essential in treating shockable cardiac rhythms. Prompt use of the AED increases the chance of return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) and survival.

(Option 2). Stopping resuscitation is inappropriate because the client is in cardiac arrest and requires immediate intervention.

(Option 3). CPR should be continued, but once an AED is available, it must be used immediately as it is the priority intervention.

(Option 4). Checking for a pulse for 30 seconds is excessive and delays treatment; pulse checks should be brief (no more than 10 seconds).

Educational Objective
In cardiac arrest, early defibrillation with an AED is critical. Once an AED becomes available, it should be used immediately to improve survival chances.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Emergency and Resuscitation Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Use the AED
Stop the resuscitation efforts
Perform CPR until emergency medical services arrives
Check for a pulse for 30 seconds before continuing CPR

standards.description: Physiological Adaptation

questionList.questionText: A nurse provides instructions to a client with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) about the positions that are most effective in alleviating dyspnea. Which statement by the client indicates a need for further instruction?
questionList.explanationText:
Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is characterized by airflow limitation, air trapping, and hyperinflation, which increase the work of breathing. Clients with COPD benefit from positions that promote maximum lung expansion, diaphragmatic descent, and use of accessory muscles. Positions such as tripod positioning, sitting upright, or leaning forward help reduce dyspnea by improving ventilation and reducing pressure on the diaphragm. In contrast, positions that restrict chest expansion or impair diaphragmatic movement can worsen breathing difficulty.

(Correct Option B). Lying on the right side in bed is incorrect because it can limit chest expansion and diaphragmatic movement, thereby worsening dyspnea. Side-lying positions do not facilitate optimal lung expansion in COPD clients and may increase discomfort and breathing effort. Therefore, this statement indicates a need for further instruction.

(Option A). Sitting up in a recliner is appropriate because it promotes upright posture, lung expansion, and ease of breathing. This position reduces pressure on the diaphragm and helps improve oxygenation.

(Option C). Sitting on the side of the bed and leaning on an overbed table is known as the tripod position, which is highly effective in COPD. It enhances the use of accessory muscles and improves ventilation efficiency.

(Option D). Standing with the back against a wall and leaning slightly forward also supports chest expansion and accessory muscle use. This position can help relieve dyspnea and is considered beneficial.

Educational Objective
Clients with COPD should be taught positions that enhance lung expansion and diaphragmatic movement, such as sitting upright or leaning forward. Positions that restrict breathing, such as lying flat or on the side, should be avoided.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)

answerChoiceList.choice: I should sit up in my recliner.
I should lie on my right side in bed.
I should sit on the side of my bed and lean on the overbed table.
I should stand with my back and hips against the wall and my shoulders bent slightly forward.

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Basic Care and Comfort

questionList.questionText: The wife of a client with angina pectoris calls the physician’s office and reports to the nurse that her husband is experiencing chest pain and has taken 2 sublingual nitroglycerin tablets 5 minutes apart, with no relief. The nurse tells the client’s wife to:
questionList.explanationText:
Angina pectoris is caused by myocardial ischemia due to reduced blood flow to the heart muscle. Nitroglycerin works by causing vasodilation, which decreases myocardial oxygen demand and improves coronary blood flow. The standard protocol for managing acute angina includes administering one sublingual nitroglycerin tablet every 5 minutes, up to a maximum of three doses. If chest pain persists after the third dose, it may indicate unstable angina or myocardial infarction, which is a medical emergency requiring immediate intervention.

(Correct Option D). The client should be instructed to take a third nitroglycerin tablet because the protocol allows up to three doses at 5-minute intervals. If there is still no relief after the third dose, this suggests a possible acute coronary syndrome, and emergency services (ambulance) should be activated immediately to ensure rapid transport and advanced care.

(Option A). Taking the client immediately to the emergency department is premature before completing the full nitroglycerin protocol. The standard guideline requires administering up to three doses before determining lack of response.

(Option B). Advising rest and waiting delays appropriate emergency care and can be dangerous. Persistent chest pain despite nitroglycerin may indicate worsening ischemia or infarction.

(Option C). Waiting for the physician to call back wastes critical time in a potentially life-threatening situation. Immediate action is necessary if chest pain is not relieved after nitroglycerin administration.

Educational Objective
Clients with angina should take up to three sublingual nitroglycerin doses at 5-minute intervals. If chest pain persists after the third dose, it is a medical emergency, and emergency services must be activated immediately.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Angina Pectoris

answerChoiceList.choice: Take her husband to the emergency department (ED) immediately
Have her husband rest and, if no relief is obtained, call back
Discuss the situation with the physician, who will call her as soon as he gets into the office
Give her husband a third tablet and, if no relief is obtained, call an ambulance to have him transported to the ED

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: Buck extension traction is applied to the right leg of a client who sustained a right hip fracture. Which intervention should the nurse include in the plan of care?
questionList.explanationText:
Buck extension traction is a type of skin traction used to immobilize fractures, reduce muscle spasms, and relieve pain before surgical repair. Unlike skeletal traction, it does not involve pins but instead uses adhesive materials, foam boots, and weights applied to the skin. Because traction is applied directly to the skin, there is a high risk for skin breakdown, pressure injury, and impaired circulation. Therefore, frequent skin assessment, neurovascular checks, and maintaining proper alignment are essential components of nursing care. The weights must remain continuously applied to maintain therapeutic effectiveness, and the nurse should avoid any actions that disrupt traction.

(Correct Option D). Checking the skin integrity of the affected leg at least every 8 hours is essential because Buck traction places continuous pressure on the skin. Early identification of redness, irritation, or breakdown helps prevent complications such as pressure ulcers and infection. Frequent assessment ensures safe and effective care while maintaining traction.

(Option A). Assessing pin sites is incorrect because Buck traction is skin traction and does not involve pins. Pin site care is only relevant in skeletal traction.

(Option B). Removing traction weights is inappropriate because traction must be continuous to be effective. Interrupting traction can cause muscle spasms and misalignment of the fracture.

(Option C). Applying lanolin is not recommended because it may soften the skin excessively and increase the risk of breakdown. Skin should be kept clean and dry rather than coated with substances that may trap moisture.

Educational Objective
In Buck extension (skin) traction, the priority is to maintain continuous traction and monitor skin integrity closely. Regular assessment helps prevent pressure injuries and ensures effective treatment.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Traction and Fracture Care

answerChoiceList.choice: Assessing the pin sites at least every 8 hours
Removing the traction weights to provide skin care
Applying lanolin to the skin of the right leg once per shift
Checking the skin integrity of the right leg at least every 8 hours

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Basic Care and Comfort

questionList.questionText: The nurse is administering cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) to an adult client. Which compression-ventilation ratio is correct?
questionList.explanationText:
Cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) is an emergency life-saving procedure performed in cases of cardiac arrest to maintain circulation and oxygenation. The goal is to provide effective chest compressions that generate blood flow to vital organs and adequate ventilations to oxygenate the blood. According to current American Heart Association (AHA) guidelines, high-quality CPR emphasizes a compression rate of 100–120 compressions per minute, adequate depth, and minimal interruptions. For adults, the standard compression-to-ventilation ratio ensures optimal balance between circulation and oxygen delivery.

(Correct Option D). The correct compression-to-ventilation ratio for adult CPR is 30:2. This ratio applies whether there is one rescuer or two rescuers. It ensures sufficient compressions to maintain circulation while allowing periodic ventilations to provide oxygen.

(Option A). A ratio of 15:1 is incorrect and not supported by current guidelines. It does not provide adequate ventilations or compressions for effective adult CPR.

(Option B). A ratio of 15:2 is used in infant and child CPR with two rescuers, not in adults. Therefore, it is inappropriate in this scenario.

(Option C). A ratio of 20:2 is not a recommended standard in any CPR guidelines and does not reflect evidence-based practice.

Educational Objective
For adult CPR, the recommended compression-to-ventilation ratio is 30:2, ensuring effective circulation and oxygenation during resuscitation efforts.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation (CPR)

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: A nurse is monitoring a client who has just undergone radical neck dissection. The nurse notes that the client’s blood pressure has dropped from 132/84 to 90/50 mm Hg and that the pulse has increased from 78 to 96 beats/min. On the basis of these findings, the nurse immediately:
questionList.explanationText:
Radical neck dissection is a major surgical procedure often performed for head and neck cancers, involving removal of lymph nodes and surrounding structures. Postoperatively, clients are at high risk for complications such as hemorrhage, hypovolemia, and shock. A sudden drop in blood pressure (hypotension) combined with an increase in heart rate (tachycardia) are classic signs of early hypovolemic shock, often due to internal or external bleeding. This condition is life-threatening and requires immediate medical intervention to prevent further deterioration and organ damage.

(Correct Option B). Contacting the physician immediately is the priority because the client is showing signs of hemodynamic instability and possible hemorrhage. Prompt notification ensures rapid medical evaluation and intervention, such as fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion, or return to surgery if needed.

(Option A). Suctioning is not appropriate because there is no indication of airway obstruction or secretion buildup. The priority concern is circulatory compromise, not airway clearance.

(Option C). Obtaining a pulse oximeter may provide additional data about oxygenation, but it does not address the urgent issue of hypotension and potential shock. Immediate intervention is required.

(Option D). Increasing the IV fluid rate may be beneficial, but it should be done with a prescription or direction from the physician. The nurse must first report the critical findings to ensure appropriate and safe management.

Educational Objective
A sudden drop in blood pressure with an increased pulse after surgery indicates possible hypovolemic shock. The nurse must recognize this as an emergency and immediately notify the physician for rapid intervention.
questionList.subject: Medical-Surgical Nursing
questionList.title: Postoperative Complications

answerChoiceList.choice: Suction the client
Contact the physician
Obtain a pulse oximeter
Increase the rate of the client’s intravenous (IV) solution

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential

questionList.questionText: A nurse has been assigned to care for an infant with tetralogy of Fallot. The infant suddenly exhibits rapid, deep respirations, irritability, and cyanosis. The nurse determines that the infant is experiencing a hypercyanotic episode and immediately:
questionList.explanationText:
Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF) is a congenital heart defect characterized by four abnormalities: ventricular septal defect, pulmonary stenosis, overriding aorta, and right ventricular hypertrophy. These defects result in right-to-left shunting, leading to decreased oxygenation of blood. A hypercyanotic episode (tet spell) occurs when there is a sudden increase in right-to-left shunting, causing severe hypoxia, cyanosis, irritability, and tachypnea. Immediate intervention is required to increase systemic vascular resistance, reduce shunting, and improve oxygenation.

(Correct Option C). Placing the infant in the knee-chest position increases systemic vascular resistance, which helps decrease the right-to-left shunt and improves pulmonary blood flow. This position is the first-line immediate intervention during a hypercyanotic episode and helps improve oxygen saturation.

(Option A). Calling a code is unnecessary as the first action. The priority is to immediately manage the episode with positioning to improve oxygenation before escalating care.

(Option B). Holding the infant upright does not effectively increase systemic vascular resistance and will not adequately relieve the hypoxic episode.

(Option D). Contacting respiratory therapy may be helpful later, but it does not address the immediate need to reduce shunting and improve oxygenation during the acute episode.

Educational Objective
During a hypercyanotic (tet) spell in tetralogy of Fallot, the priority intervention is to place the infant in the knee-chest position to increase systemic vascular resistance and improve oxygenation.
questionList.subject: Pediatric Nursing
questionList.title: Congenital Heart Defects

answerChoiceList.choice: Calls a code
Holds the infant in an upright position
Places the infant in the knee-chest position
Contacts the respiratory therapy department

standards.description: Physiological Integrity: Reduction of Risk Potential
